speaker: interviewer
text: It's April the 11th, 1996. I'm Susan [? Franzen ?] interviewing Paul Oppenheimer in Solihull, England. Paul, Would you like to introduce your family to me please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will, thank you. This is the most important one. This is my wife, Corinne. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my oldest son, Nicholas. And this is my daughter, Judith. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is-- this is my sister Eve.

speaker: interviewer
text: I consider my-- the most important. Also-- also called Rachel, the most important one, yes, in terms of this story. Quite true, Eve. And finally, we have Julie, who is the wife of Nicholas, and three weeks old Alexander, son of Nicholas and Julie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, lovely. Julie, can you just turn--

speaker: interviewee
text: Turn him round.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just for a moment, so we can have a good shot of him.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's just he's--

speaker: interviewee
text: See how grumpy he is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: There we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello, there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oh, dear.

speaker: interviewee
text: He must be one of your youngest stars.

speaker: interviewer
text: He-- he certainly is. He-- he has been the youngest. Eve, I'll ask you first of all, is there anything you would like to say to Paul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he's been a very good brother. He's looked after me fairly well. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, we keep in touch now quite often. And it's always a pleasure to be here with-- with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nick, I'll ask you. How did you find out that your father was a Holocaust survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be a long, long time ago. Well, I'm 31 now, I think perhaps I first became aware of it, I was told, perhaps when I was as young as eight or nine, I think. I would have been aware of that. But perhaps the-- understanding what that meant, perhaps didn't come until I was in my early teens. And it was perhaps through Jewish education and through talking with my mother and my father's closest family that I've probably learnt most of the stories. I think it's fair to say that Dad didn't talk about it a great deal himself [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about you, Judith? When did you find out and how?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mom tells me I knew from about five. But obviously, I didn't really understand what war was or anything like that. So I'd say about the same, when you sort of really understood, again in the teenage years. You know, understanding what the Holocaust was and reading more about it and finding out more, which I'm still doing. I'm finding out a lot and still, you know, thirsty to find out more, see what it was all about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you reacted when you heard that he was a survivor, when you first had an understanding of what it meant?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very difficult. I don't remember an actual point, an actual moment, where suddenly, you know, I understood. But there were certain points where things would become clearer or understood a bit more about feeling like missing grandparents or understanding what it would have been like to be in a camp or something like that through reading or-- or talking to people or-- or hearing from Dad or Mom, again, going to Belsen with them. I suppose there are points where it becomes clearer. Not necessarily more understandable, but sort of, learn a bit more. I don't know it that makes any sense.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say a bit more what it is was like going to Belsen with your father and your uncle.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was difficult. I think-- I think it's really important to go. I had this sense of being more involved in a youth movement of finding out more about the Holocaust. I sort of really wanted to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was, sort of-- I was glad that he said that I could go with him. I don't know that I expected it to be anything. And it was at moments very emotional, at other times very, again, informative just to have been there. But I-- I don't know that I understood any more about what it was about. But I was there with Dad, so that was important enough. Have you gone back, Nick, or gone there to Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I haven't been back to any of the-- any of the places where these events happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know I'd like to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking back over your childhood and teenage years and comparing yourself with friends, would you say your life has been any different having a-- having had a survivor for a parent?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd say yes in a very positive way, in that, sort of, knowing what happened to my father or what happened to the Jewish people and that I had an involvement or a connection to that, meant that it was-- it becomes very difficult to to hate other people or-- or to abide racism or prejudice of many kind. So yeah, I think that's something that you feel. Because my father was persecuted against. My grandparents died. I'm not going to do that to somebody else. I'm not going to let people around me do that. So I think there's that, that's been, sort of, quite a major part. I know that's kind of forefront. And when people say things anti-Semitic or otherwise, you know, you really do feel it, and you understand the significance, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that's right. There is-- these are significant events that are taking place, even in our lifetime, where-- where peoples are-- are being persecuted. And it becomes there, but for the grace of God, we'd be ourselves again. But I think also a very tangible thing is not having-- Judith mentions, you know, grandparents. And clearly, her and I friends tended to have, you know, grandparents on both sides of their families. And I think that's something that perhaps we've missed without knowing that we-- there was something missing there. Because they're-- you know, they're people, that we-- family, that we just have very little knowledge of even, really.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, there are people who don't have grandparents or who died before they were born or whatever, but our grandparents were murdered in-- in the Holocaust. You know, there's something different. And you have to explain that or say what that's about. Or, you know, just knowing that, I think, is-- is something a little different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Corinne, turning to you, is there something that you'd like to say to Paul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think it's very important that he's done what he's done today and during the last five years, I think the work that he's been doing and his involvement. Because he's found it important to him and to the Jewish Nation and, in fact, to everyone, that it's very important that he's done it. And I think we'll be very proud to-- to show our grandchildren. This is the first of many, I hope. Thanks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Julie, is there anything that you'd like to say to Paul and to the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, obviously, before I was married to Nick, I was aware of the Holocaust and that. But sort of talking to Paul and also to [? Rutzi, ?] Paul's brother, has made-- sort of, has given me a greater awareness. And I think that's-- it's important that everybody should know about that, you know, not just, sort of, families of survivors. But it's important that everybody should be aware of exactly what's happened. And I'm-- I'm glad, sort of, to hear Paul's story. And I'd like Alex, as well, in the future to, sort of, have a full awareness of what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you very much, indeed, all of you for coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a copy of my birth certificate. You can see that it was Berlin-Charlottenburg, that it was the 20th of September, 1928, and that my name was Paul Friedrich Oppenheimer. Now, this is something that I did not actually mention during the talk. But I started off as Friedrich, and at the end of the war, I conveniently forgot the Friedrich, because I thought it was rather Germanic. So now, I'm just called Paul Oppenheimer. My father was Doctor of Philosophy, Hans Felix Robert Oppenheimer. And Friederike Oppenheimer, born first, was my mother, both living in Berlin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is obviously a copy, which we obtained in 1989. This is my father, and this is my mother. Johann Felix Robert Oppenheimer, Friederike Oppenheimer, born first, both Doctors of Philosophy. And I would say that this was taken in Germany during the 1920s maybe just before or after their marriage, which was about 1926. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Thank you. This is my mother with my younger brother Rudy. And I am watching the camera on the right-hand side. Paul on the right, Rudy, lying down, my mother sitting on the balcony of our house in Berlin, I would say. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is another photograph of me with my favorite toy elephant. And it's actually my favorite photograph, taken by a professional, Fritz [? Henlein ?] in Berlin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I am the one on the left and two very Germanic looking friends next to me. The big cornets of sweets were a sign of the first day at school. On that occasion, we all had one of these big cornets. So both the girl and me were obviously our first days, not the other chap, who had the nice hat, obviously a prospective Nazi.

speaker: interviewer
text: About when would this had been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: First day at school, so I would say '35, 1935, at the age of six or seven. This is my class in the state school in 1935, when I was six or seven years old. You'll see me there amongst a lot of Germans. I presume-- probably, I was the only Jew in the class. Certainly, the two either side of me don't look Jewish. The other interesting thing is at the very back of the class was a photograph of Adolf Hitler. And also, the red, white, and black flag is hanging there. The photograph of Adolf Hitler at the back of the class was scratched out, I think, by one of my children at one time. But they all deny it. That was Adolf Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which your children have scratched out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which my children-- blotted out. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And there's the flag.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the flag red white, and black. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a report of the class that you've just seen the photograph of in the half year 1935. This is me, Paul Oppenheimer born 29/9/28. Perhaps you ought to see the funny Germanic script that we had to learn at that time. And generally, my marks seem to be two, which is good, and three, which is sufficient. And it says how many hours I missed, six. And this is signed by my father, Dr. Hans Oppenheimer there. And these are the various teachers, the stamp from Berlin, Stadt Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come by these reports?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Well, these were all amongst the documents that we must have salvaged from Berlin, taken to Holland. My parents had them. And obviously, all of these documents and photographs that we collected from those days were placed with friends in Holland, who kept them for us during the war. And after the war, we went back-- I went back to collect them all. There were boxes and suitcases full of stuff. I mean, for example, I've got an electric train set, a Marklin, a German electric train set, from before the war that was salvaged in the same way. And the like, a camera that was dated 1936. So we bought it in Berlin. My father bought it, and we still have it today. It was all given to good Dutch friends in Holland. And they kept these things for us, and we salvaged them after the war. And I've kept them ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what year did you go back to retrieve them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably straight after the war. I-- I-- I wouldn't be surprised if they gave them to us, more or less, immediately when we came back. Because there were a lot of clothes, as well, that we had back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the famous birth certificate of my sister, Rachel Eve Dorothy Oppenheimer born in Hendon, London on the 23rd of June, 1936. This is a photostat copy given in 1954, but we had earlier versions of that with us throughout our time in the camps. This is basically the document that saved us from being sent to Auschwitz or Sobibor. Here is a happy photograph of our family, my mother with Rudy and Eve and me on the left. This is in [NON-ENGLISH]. We lived in a house similar to the one across the road. That, across the road, is actually where the Miens lived who were exchanged to Palestine from Belsen. This is our drive. We didn't have a car, but we had bicycles. And this was a funny contraption that I'm sitting on, which is called a [NON-ENGLISH], a Flying Dutchman. It's a bit like a soap box. And you pull this thing backwards and forwards to make it move, and you can steer with your feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rachel is on the rocking horse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachel is on a sort of rocking horse type of pram kind of arrangement, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Rudy's with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's like an old fashioned go-kart.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sort of. This is the identity card that we had to have in Holland to make sure that they knew which of us were Jews. This was introduced before we actually had the stars. In this case, we have two identity cards. This one is the front of my grandmother, Meta. And this is the back of the identity card of my grandfather, Josef. The interesting thing is that, first of all, by the side of the photograph is the big J to denote Jew. There are also fingerprints. And then, by the name Josef in-- typewritten, there is also handwritten later on in ink, Israel, which all the Jews in Holland and occupied countries, they had to add the name Israel for males and Sara for females. So in this case, Sara is on the other side. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There is the Sara added to the name Meta. This is my grandfather, my grandmother's fingerprint. [SPEAKING DUTCH], which means identity card in Dutch. This was in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandmother's fingerprints.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Issued in 1941, November 1941. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, this is the famous star, the yellow star with the word 'Jew' in Dutch imprinted on it. And these were the kind of stars that we had to wear whenever we were outside our home in public to identify us and to make sure that we didn't transgress any of the many laws about Jews. This is an actual star that did belong to me and I had on my clothes, presumably all the time in Westerbork and Belsen, and on the train, et cetera, et cetera. And it's survived pretty well. It is a bit flimsy, but it's done all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you want to keep it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am a natural hoarder. This is another thing that I forgot to mention earlier on. I tend to keep everything. We keep plastic bags. And we obviously, keep things like that, yes. I mean, we have stamp collections also from before the war, now that you mention it. Although, I'm not really a stamp collector these days, but my Uncle Rudy, especially, was a big stamp collector and my father also. And yeah, I'm used to collecting. And I tend to hoard everything. And I certainly wouldn't throw away things like that. I didn't think they would ever be famous or worth a lot of money of anything like that. But memorabilia, maybe it'll help me to remind or help our-- our children to remind us of those days. It's nice to have the original one because there are not all that many, I suppose, around anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So amongst the various possessions, which we had in Holland and which we gave to friends to look after whilst we were away and hopefully, to recover after the war is, for example, this Leica camera, which I believe is dated about 1936. And it's still in working order. We have used it occasionally.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my family complains, because they have to stand still for about 10 minutes while I focus it. And by that time, they've disappeared. We have thought about selling it occasionally. But perhaps we shouldn't. Perhaps we'd better keep it as a family heirloom. I hadn't realized its significance with regard to the war and the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Another item that we left with friends in Holland and recovered after the war is a Marklin zero zero, 00, train set. This is the piece. This is the engine, slightly battered. And this is one of the carriages, blue car. And here is one of the tracks points.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we have the complete set, and it probably is just about working with difficulty. I must admit that we haven't played with it for ages. We've waited for the arrival of the grandson. Now that Alexander is here, we'll soon be able to start playing with it again. So we now come to Westerbork. And this is my arbeitskarte, my working card. You will see my name and date of birth and the barrack number, 68.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the other side, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] more difficult to read, is the date, for example, 17/8/1943, 2T, which means two days. And then D FR, which means [NON-ENGLISH], doesn't have to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there some kind of signature behind it? Maybe or maybe not. But basically most of those days are [NON-ENGLISH] or BR which is [NON-ENGLISH]. I have to rest in bed one day, two days, two days, et cetera. And so you can see all the dates when we were in Westerbork and the number of days that I didn't have to work, because I had the odd cold or cough or sneeze. There is a sort of signature behind it, isn't it? The highlighted one, three days, BR, and then there's a signature of a doctor or initials of a doctor. Initials.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't ask me who that was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is also from Westerbork, my badekarte, my bath card. And it shows that in the months of December and January my pre-arranged date was Friday at 1500 hours. So 3 o'clock in the afternoon, it was my duty to present myself for being bathed. Dated 15 December 1943.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the other side-- and on the back of the badekarte, they've actually stamped the card to say that I did have my bath, 24th of December, 1st of January 1944, 7th of January. That can't be right, 1st and 7th. 14th of January 1944, 21st of January, 28th of January 1944. And on the 1st of February, three days after that, we were deported to Bergen-Belsen. So those were the last days in Westerbork. My last bath there, probably for quite a long time, 28th of January 1944. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the top half of a very simple letter, which was sent by my mother Friederike Oppenheimer, to a Dr. Prager in Amsterdam asking him to send syrup-- Famel I think is the name of this cough syrup-- and some pajamas for my husband. So this is the bottom half of the same letter, which has the signature Oppenheimer there of-- looks more like my father than my mother, actually, saying that the letter was posted 24th of 11 in Westerbork. In Westerbork, we actually had our own money. This is Lager Westerbork Gutschein, which is in effect like money. It looked like monopoly type of money. That's worth $0.25. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, there's nothing more. We-- we could use the money within the camps to buy things. There were shops where you could buy maybe food and various other things. Or maybe you had to pay for the cinema or whatever. And this was the money. Because we had to hand in all our real money when we arrived in Westerbork. And presumably, we got these sort of things, these bits of paper, in exchange. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is an example of a letter that we've recovered after the war, which was sent by my mother from barracks number 65 in Westerbork on the 23rd of January 1942. And the stamp is on it. The message-- part of the message is here. There's her signature, Friederike. The other message-- the rest of the message is on the other side. This is preprinted stationery that you could buy, presumably, in Westerbork. It says Lager Westerbork in two places. And everything was censored, of course. So it was proofed, censored. I just would like to correct-- this date is actually her birth date, born 23rd of January, 1902 not the date of posting, which we might be able to decipher from the stamp if you could read it, but anyway, sometime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whilst we were in Westerbork. And you can see, it is to [INAUDIBLE]. That was her official title, Mrs. S. Koster Vecht [INAUDIBLE] 1631. We lived at Vecht [INAUDIBLE] 1632, just above her in Amsterdam, [NON-ENGLISH] Zed, Z in American.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date looked like the 23 of December, 1943.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's quite possible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is when we were evacuated from Bergen-Belsen. There is Bergen-Belsen. And on the 9th of April, 1945, we were put into one of these trains, the third train, in Bergen-Belsen. On the 10th of April, we left Bergen-Belsen, and we went on in this direction. On that same day, the 10th of April, this is where the British Army was. This is where the front was of the British Army. They were within 20, 25 miles of liberating us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So our train journey went just ahead of the British Army all the while, like this, like that. We had difficulties here crossing the rivers, because many of the bridges had been blown. And this is why we zig-zagged around quite a lot. And we went like this, that, and then right through the middle of Berlin on the 19th of April.

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time, Belsen had been liberated on the 15th of April. And the front, was-- on the 18th of April, was here. So at that time, when the front was there, we were here. And now, the Russians were coming from the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So our train turned south. We wandered along here. And this is where we were liberated on the 23rd of April, near Trobitz, which is somewhere here. And here is Torgau, where two days later, the Russian Army and the American Army met here at Torgau by the river Elbe. And we were here. Did you know the date when you started your journey, or did you find that out subsequently, looking backwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we knew quite a lot about the dates. I never knew how we knew the date that my mother died and my father died. And this is a-- a mystery kind of thing how we always knew what the date was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because I don't think there were calendars.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't-- well, there might have been the odd one, because we did have our possessions with us. And somebody might have worked out what-- what the dates were, especially the religious people for their festivals and all that sort of thing. So yes, we did seem to know what the dates were.
